content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 4, 1994

Daniel S. Hentschke
City Attorney
City of Oceanside
300 North Hill Street
Oceanside, CA 92054

Re: Your Request For Advice
Our File No. I-94-104

Dear Mr. Hentschke:

We respond to your request for advice on behalf of Oceanside
City Councilmember Colleen O'Harra. You have requested informal
advice. Under the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹, a request
for informal advice does not provide immunity for the requestor.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18259(c)(3)).

QUESTION

May Councilmember O'Harra, who also serves as a director of
the Oceanside Harbor District, make, participate in, or influence
a governmental decision concerning the harbor district and the
businesses therein when one of those businesses is a client of the
law firm which is a source of income to her?

CONCLUSION

The fact that the client of the law firm is not a source of
income to Councilmember O'Harra does not end the analysis. A
conflict may still exist if the decision has certain effects on
the law firm, which is a source of income (source of income)
to the councilmember.

If the decision has the foreseeable effect of increasing or
decreasing the income, assets, or liabilities of the law firm by
the requisite amount, then the councilmember would be prohibited
from participating in the decision. (Regulation 18702.2).

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to regulations to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-94-104
Page 2


FACTS

The following facts are summarized from your request letter.
The Commission does not act as a fact finder in providing advice.
(In re Ogelsby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

Councilmember Colleen O'Harra was elected to the Oceanside
City Council in November 1992. In her capacity as a member of the
city council, she also serves as a member of the Oceanside Small
Craft Harbor Districts (the "District"). The District is a
governmental entity existing pursuant to the provisions of the
California Harbors and Navigation Code. By statute, the city
council is the ex officio district board.

Councilmember O'Harra is a member of the California Bar and
practices in association with the Oceanside law firm of Core,
Grosse, Greenman & Lacy (the "Firm"). Councilmember O'Harra's
salary from the Firm is based upon a percentage of the gross
income received from her billings alone. Although she is
guaranteed a certain base salary, the compensation based upon the
percentage of her gross billings has always exceeded the base
salary. Councilmember O'Harra does not share in the profits of
the Firm as a whole. The Firm pays the cost of professional
insurance, secretarial service, office space, library use, and
other office expenses. Councilmember O'Harra generates
approximately ninety-five percent of her client base herself; the
remaining five percent is referred to her by the Firm.

Previous Commission advice to Councilmember O'Harra held that
both the Firm and the clients of the councilmember were sources of
income under the Act. (O'Harra Advice Letter, No. I-92-660).2

Recently, the Firm has commenced an attorney-client
relationship with a business (the "Client") located within the
boundaries of the District. The Client-leases property from
Seajet, the master tenant, who leases the property from the
District. The Client has retained the Firm with respect to its
dealings with Seajet.

Under the terms of the master lease, the District has a
limited authority to approve the terms and conditions of the
subleases. However, once a sublease is executed, the
responsibility for management of the sublease belongs to Seajet.
The Client's sublease will come before the District for approval.

Moreover, the master tenant, Seajet, has defaulted on the
terms of the master lease. The District has declared a default
and has declared a forfeiture of the base lease. Further action

2  The advice and analysis contained in the Commission's
previous advice letter, No. I-92-660, is hereby incorporated by
this reference. Copies of the O'Harra Advice Letter, No. I-92-
660, are available upon request from the Commission.

File No. A-94-104
Page 3


to regain possession of the premises has not been undertaken but
may be undertaken in the future.

ANALYSIS

     Public officials are prohibited from making, participating
in, or influencing decisions in which they know or have reason to
know that they have a financial interest. (Section 87100).  A
public official has a financial interest in a decision when it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, different from its effect on the public
generally, on, among other things, a source of income of two
hundred and fifty dollars or more during the twelve month period
prior to the decision.  (Section 87103.)3

     You have properly identified the Firm as a source of income
to the councilmember.  The decisions in question directly affect
the Client of the Firm.  The conflict analysis, however, depends
on the decision's effect on the Firm, the identified financial
interest.  We note that the law firm itself is not the subject of
the referenced decisions.  Further, it does not appear that the
law firm initiates the referenced proceedings before the District.
Therefore, any effect on the law firm is indirect by definition.
(Regulation 18702.1(b)).

     Decisions which indirectly affect a business entity
identified as a financial interest of a public official are
material when they have the effect of increasing or decreasing the
gross revenue, assets, liabilities, or expenses of the business
entity by the relevant amounts identified in Regulation 18702.2.

     Assuming, for the purpose of example only, that the Firm is
covered under subsection (g) of Regulation 18702.2, the
councilmember would have a conflict if the effect of the decision
would result in: (1) an increase or decrease in the Firm's gross
revenues for a fiscal year of ten thousand dollars or more; or (2)
the Firm incurring, avoiding, reducing, or eliminating expenses of
two thousand five hundred dollars or more in a fiscal year; or,
finally, (3) an increase or decrease in the Firm's assets or
liabilities of ten thousand dollars or more.

     Accordingly, if the decision to approve the Client's sublease
is one which will have the reasonably foreseeable effect of
increasing or decreasing the Firm's gross revenue, expenses,
assets, or liabilities, Regulation 18702.2 should be referenced to
determine if the effect is material.  If the effect is material,

3         The foreseeability of a financial effect must necessarily
depend on the facts of each case.  An effect would be reasonably
foreseeable when there is a substantial likelihood that it will
occur.  Certainty is not required.  However, if an effect is only
a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.  (In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198).

File No. A-94-104
Page 4


and is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
Councilmember O'Harra would have a conflict under the Act which
would prohibit her from making, participating in, or influencing
the decision.

Decisions which concern the defaulting master tenant may be
examined in the same manner. For example, assuming, that the
sublease contains some type of subrogation or attornment clause
that would allow the District to step into the shoes of Basket
vis-a-vis the Client, with no net change in the terms of sublease,
then a decision to evict the master tenant would probably result
in no reasonably foreseeable effect on the Firm. However, should
the forfeiture of the master lease have the effect of invalidating
the Client's sublease, it may or may not be reasonably
foreseeable, depending on the facts, that the Client would become
involved in renegotiation or collateral litigation which may
result in a material effect on the Firm, an identified financial
interest of the councilmember.

We emphasize that conflict of interest analysis is extremely
fact specific and the examples used in this letter are for
illustrative purposes only.

We hope that this analysis aids Councilmember O'Harra in the
proper execution of her public duties. Should your office or
those you serve require additional information, please do not
hesitate to contact the undersigned at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel,

By: Daniel E. Muallem
    Counsel, Legal Division
